gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The COVID-19 pandemic created an urgent need for effective treatments. Structure-based drug design via docking can accelerate drug repurposing and new drug development against SARS-CoV-2. However, virtual screening success depends on accurate target structures and consideration of protein flexibility and genetic variations. The study aims to develop DockThor-VS, a web server providing curated 3D structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (wild type and non-synonymous variants) and drug libraries, to facilitate repurposing virtual screening and assess the impact of mutations on drug binding.

section_gists.heading: Drug design and repurposing with DockThor-VS web server focusing on SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets and their non-synonym variants
section_gists.gist: Introduces COVID-19 pandemic and the need for treatments. Describes SARS-CoV-2 genome, proteins, and mutations. Highlights the role of structure-based drug design and virtual screening. Presents DockThor-VS as a web server with curated target structures and drug libraries for repurposing, incorporating non-synonymous variations. States the goal to assess impact of mutations on drug binding.
process_signals: Motivation: urgent need for COVID-19 treatments
Hypothesis: considering multiple conformations and mutations improves virtual screening
Approach: develop web server with pre-prepared targets and drug libraries

section_gists.heading: Results and discussion
section_gists.gist: Describes the dataset of potential therapeutic targets: Nsp3 (PLpro), Nsp5 (Mpro), Nsp12 (RdRp), Nsp15 (NendoU), N protein, and Spike. For each, provides structural details, flexibility considerations, and selected conformations. Discusses virtual screening results of FDA-approved drugs against these targets, including top-scoring compounds and mutation effects. Highlights the importance of using multiple conformations to avoid false negatives.
process_signals: Observation: protein flexibility (e.g., Tyr268 in PLpro, Met49/Asn142/Gln189 in Mpro) affects binding
Decision: provide multiple conformations per target
Result: top drug candidates identified; some show consistent scores across variants, others vary
Partial result: some drugs score well only in certain conformations or variants
methods: Target preparation: protonation states assigned based on literature mechanisms
Docking: virtual screening of FDA-approved drugs using DockThor-VS
Mutation analysis: in silico point mutations and docking
metrics: Docking scores (e.g., Table 2 shows affinity predictions for Spike variants)
failures_or_limits: Some drugs show variable scores across variants, indicating potential resistance
Limited to available crystal structures; some targets (e.g., Macrodomain) not yet included

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Details the analysis of non-synonymous variations using GISAID and CoV-GLUE, with impact prediction via PROVEAN. Describes protein structure preparation using Maestro's Protein Preparation Wizard, including protonation, hydrogen-bond optimization, and visual inspection. In silico mutations performed with Modeller. Docking protocol uses DockThor with grid box centered on binding site, and scoring function.
process_signals: Constraint: need to select representative conformations from NMR ensembles (e.g., N protein)
Decision: cluster open conformations to avoid steric clashes
Method: use literature-guided protonation states for catalytic residues
methods: Non-synonymous variation analysis: GISAID, CoV-GLUE, PROVEAN
Protein preparation: Maestro Protein Preparation Wizard, PROPKA, visual inspection
Docking: DockThor with grid box, scoring function

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Summarizes the development of DockThor-VS for repurposing drugs against six SARS-CoV-2 proteins with wild type and variant structures. Emphasizes the importance of multiple conformations to reduce false negatives. Notes that mutations can alter drug interaction profiles, aiding broader-spectrum drug design. Acknowledges continuous updates with new structures and methods. Suggests consensus scoring with other programs.
process_signals: Validation: importance of multiple conformations confirmed
Limitation: need for continuous updates as new structures emerge
Future: incorporate new docking methodologies and protein flexibility strategies
failures_or_limits: Study limited to six targets; other targets to be added
Docking scores are approximate; consensus scoring recommended

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The researchers hypothesized that providing pre-prepared, curated structures of SARS-CoV-2 targets, including multiple conformations and common variants, would enable more effective virtual screening for drug repurposing.
They believed that considering protein flexibility and mutations is crucial to avoid false negatives and to identify drugs with broad-spectrum potential.
The study aimed to demonstrate the utility of the DockThor-VS platform by performing virtual screening of FDA-approved drugs and analyzing mutation impacts.

events.summary: The COVID-19 pandemic created an urgent need for effective treatments, and virtual screening success depends on factors like target structure preparation, compound library preparation, receptor flexibility, and genomic variations.
Developed DockThor-VS web server to provide curated structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets and drug libraries for repurposing virtual screening.
For each protein target, chose a set of structures representing different relevant conformations to account for receptor flexibility.
Protonation states of ionisable residues were carefully investigated in the available literature for each protein target.
Repurposing libraries were carefully prepared considering different protonation states and tautomerism for each compound.
Macrocycle drugs were subjected to special treatment to generate distinct conformations.
Provided two PLpro structures (PDB 6W9C and 6WX4) to represent flexibility of Tyr268 and Leu162.

events.summary: Superposition of Mpro structures reveals significant conformational changes in residues Met49, Asn142 and Gln189, reinforcing the importance of multiple conformations in virtual screening.
Mpro structures prepared with manually adjusted protonation states: neutral His41 at ND1, catalytic Cys145 protonated, neutral His163 at NE2, neutral His164 at NE2.
Nsp15 structure prepared based on conformation complexed with tipiracil (PDB 6WXC), with specific protonation states for catalytic residues.
Five NMR conformations of N-NTD provided to account for protein flexibility, selected by clustering based on Arg92 and Tyr109 conformations.
Suggested binding site for docking on N-NTD centered on hotspot between finger and palm subdomains, essential for RNA binding.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk describes structural details and preparation decisions for Mpro, RdRp, Nsp15, and N protein, highlighting flexibility considerations and binding site definitions for virtual screening.

events.summary: Selection of non-synonymous variations for each target based on impact on catalytic region, ligand interaction, amino acid properties, and literature evidence.
Virtual screening of FDA-approved drugs (e-Drug3D) against all six SARS-CoV-2 targets using wild type structures and ensemble docking.
Docking scores for drugs under clinical trials against COVID-19 are presented in Table 1, showing predicted affinities across targets.
Docking results are not for clinical guidance; top hits need experimental validation.
Some drugs may inhibit multiple SARS-CoV-2 targets, exhibiting multiple antiviral mechanisms.

we present the developments implemented in the DockThor-VS web server to provide a virtual screening (VS) platform with curated structures of potential therapeutic targets from SARS-CoV-2 incorporating genetic information regarding relevant non-synonymous variations
DockThor-VS provides ready-for-docking 3D structures for wild type and selected mutations for Nsp3 (papain-like, PLpro domain), Nsp5 (Mpro, 3CLpro), Nsp12 (RdRp), Nsp15 (NendoU), N protein, and Spike

For each protein target, we chose a set of structures representing different relevant conformations complexed with varying types of ligand (e.g., ligands covalently bound or not, side chains conformations closing or opening the binding site), thus providing an ensemble of representative structures of the flexibility of the receptor.
For each protein target, the protonation states of ionisable residues were carefully investigated in the available literature.
The repurposing libraries open to the users were carefully prepared considering the different protonation states and tautomerism for each compound.
Macrocycle drugs were subjected to special treatment to generate distinct conformations.

Developed DockThor-VS web server to provide curated structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets and drug libraries for repurposing virtual screening.
For each protein target, chose a set of structures representing different relevant conformations to account for receptor flexibility.
Protonation states of ionisable residues were carefully investigated in the available literature for each protein target.
Repurposing libraries were carefully prepared considering different protonation states and tautomerism for each compound.
Macrocycle drugs were subjected to special treatment to generate distinct conformations.
Provided two PLpro structures (PDB 6W9C and 6WX4) to represent flexibility of Tyr268 and Leu162.
Superposition of Mpro structures reveals significant conformational changes in residues Met49, Asn142 and Gln189, reinforcing the importance of multiple conformations in virtual screening.
Mpro structures prepared with manually adjusted protonation states: neutral His41 at ND1, catalytic Cys145 protonated, neutral His163 at NE2, neutral His164 at NE2.
Nsp15 structure prepared based on conformation complexed with tipiracil (PDB 6WXC), with specific protonation states for catalytic residues.
Five NMR conformations of N-NTD provided to account for protein flexibility, selected by clustering based on Arg92 and Tyr109 conformations.
Suggested binding site for docking on N-NTD centered on hotspot between finger and palm subdomains, essential for RNA binding.
Selection of non-synonymous variations for each target based on impact on catalytic region, ligand interaction, amino acid properties, and literature evidence.
Virtual screening of FDA-approved drugs (e-Drug3D) against all six SARS-CoV-2 targets using wild type structures and ensemble docking.
Docking scores for drugs under clinical trials against COVID-19 are presented in Table 1, showing predicted affinities across targets.
Docking results are not for clinical guidance; top hits need experimental validation.
Some drugs may inhibit multiple SARS-CoV-2 targets, exhibiting multiple antiviral mechanisms.
Analysis of virtual screening results shows most predicted binding affinities are moderate, consistent with experimental studies showing modest inhibitory effects.

source.title: Drug design and repurposing with DockThor-VS web server focusing on SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets and their non-synonym variants

initial_request.research_problem: The COVID-19 pandemic created an urgent need for effective treatments. Virtual screening success depends on accurate target structures, receptor flexibility, and genetic variations. The study aims to develop DockThor-VS, a web server providing curated 3D structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (wild type and non-synonymous variants) and drug libraries, to facilitate repurposing virtual screening and assess the impact of mutations on drug binding.
initial_request.target_object: SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (Nsp3 PLpro, Nsp5 Mpro, Nsp12 RdRp, Nsp15 NendoU, N protein, Spike) and their non-synonymous variants
initial_request.known_context: Virtual screening success depends on target structure preparation, compound library preparation, receptor flexibility, and genomic variations. Non-specialist researchers often struggle with these factors.
constraints: Use only FDA-approved drugs from e-Drug3D library
Docking results must not be used for clinical guidance
input_data: Curated 3D structures of SARS-CoV-2 targets (wild type and selected mutations)
quantifiable_goals: Identify promising drugs under clinical trials against COVID-19
Assess impact of non-synonymous variations on drug binding

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A web server providing curated structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (wild type and non-synonymous variants) and drug libraries will facilitate repurposing virtual screening and help assess the impact of mutations on drug binding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop DockThor-VS web server.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The success of virtual screening experiments depends on factors that are often not trivial to be addressed by non-specialist researchers. Possible genomic variations in the active/binding site region of molecular targets can drastically affect the binding mode and affinity of ligands.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The identified need motivates the design of the DockThor-VS web server.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Providing curated structures of Nsp3 PLpro, Nsp5 Mpro, Nsp12 RdRp, Nsp15 NendoU, N protein, and Spike will enable effective virtual screening.

success_verification.final_conclusion: DockThor-VS provides a platform for virtual screening against SARS-CoV-2 targets, incorporating receptor flexibility and genetic variations. Ledipasvir, imatinib, lopinavir, and daclatasvir were identified as promising multi-target drugs. Multiple conformations reduce false negatives; mutations may confer resistance. Docking results are not for clinical guidance.
limitations: Docking results must not be used for clinical guidance; top hits need experimental validation.
Protein dynamic effects caused by mutations are not taken into account by the docking protocol.
Platform currently covers only six targets; continuous updates planned.
